message:
fix(fm-cli): enforce core.max_input_bytes via bounded reads on stdin and files

`load_input()` was advisory: it read the entire stdin or file into memory
with `read_to_string`, then compared the resulting String length against
`core.max_input_bytes` and bailed afterwards. That is exactly backwards —
by the time we reject a 5 GiB file we have already allocated 5 GiB. The
bound is meant to cap memory use, not describe it.

Rewrite both paths to use `Read::take(max_input_bytes + 1)` so the reader
never pulls more than `max_input_bytes + 1` bytes off the underlying source:

  - stdin: `io::stdin().take(limit + 1)` into a `String`. The `+1` lets us
    tell "fit exactly" apart from "was truncated at the limit" — if after
    reading the length still exceeds `max_input_bytes`, the input was
    actually larger and we bail with the same error as before.

  - file: open explicitly, `take(limit + 1)`, then `read_to_string`. Same
    trick for detection. We now also cannot be fooled by an initial stat
    that races with a writer growing the file between `metadata()` and the
    actual read — the read itself is the enforcement.

`u64::try_from(max_input_bytes).unwrap_or(u64::MAX).saturating_add(1)`
handles the edge case where `usize` (on 32-bit targets) would otherwise
fail the conversion, and where `max_input_bytes` could be `usize::MAX`.

The error message loses the post-decoding byte count since we no longer
have a bounded value to report — we know it exceeds the limit, not by how
much. That was the whole point.

Integration tests and the tree-overflow regression test are updated to
match the tightened behavior.
